title: Ruby (programming language)

infobox.value: [Multi-paradigm](/wiki/Multi-paradigm_programming_language "Multi-paradigm programming language"): [functional](/wiki/Functional_programming "Functional programming"), [imperative](/wiki/Imperative_programming "Imperative programming"), [object-oriented](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming"), [reflective](/wiki/Reflective_programming "Reflective programming")
4.0.2 [Edit this on Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q161053?uselang=en#P348 "Edit this on Wikidata") / 16 March 2026; 8 days ago (16 March 2026)

is a [general-purpose programming language](/wiki/General-purpose_programming_language "General-purpose programming language"). Its design puts an emphasis on programming productivity and simplicity. In Ruby, everything is an [object](/wiki/Object_(computer_science) "Object (computer science)"), including [primitive data types](/wiki/Primitive_data_type "Primitive data type"). [Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto](/wiki/Yukihiro_Matsumoto "Yukihiro Matsumoto") started to develop the language in the mid-1990s in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").

**Ruby** is a [general-purpose programming language](/wiki/General-purpose_programming_language "General-purpose programming language"). Its design puts an emphasis on programming productivity and simplicity. In Ruby, everything is an [object](/wiki/Object_(computer_science) "Object (computer science)"), including [primitive data types](/wiki/Primitive_data_type "Primitive data type"). [Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto](/wiki/Yukihiro_Matsumoto "Yukihiro Matsumoto") started to develop the language in the mid-1990s in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").

Ruby is [interpreted](/wiki/Interpreted_language "Interpreted language"), [high-level](/wiki/High-level_programming_language "High-level programming language"), and [dynamically typed](/wiki/Dynamic_typing "Dynamic typing"); its interpreter uses [garbage collection](/wiki/Garbage_collection_(computer_science) "Garbage collection (computer science)") and [just-in-time compilation](/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation "Just-in-time compilation"). It supports multiple programming paradigms, including [procedural](/wiki/Procedural_programming "Procedural programming"), [object-oriented](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming"), and [functional programming](/wiki/Functional_programming "Functional programming"). According to the creator, Ruby was influenced by [Perl](/wiki/Perl "Perl"), [Smalltalk](/wiki/Smalltalk "Smalltalk"), [Eiffel](/wiki/Eiffel_(programming_language) "Eiffel (programming language)"), [Ada](/wiki/Ada_(programming_language) "Ada (programming language)"), [BASIC](/wiki/BASIC "BASIC"), and [Lisp](/wiki/Lisp_(programming_language) "Lisp (programming language)").[[8]](#cite_note-about-8)[[2]](#cite_note-confreaks-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early concept")]

According to Matsumoto, Ruby was conceived in 1993. In a 1999 post to the Ruby-Talk mailing list, he shared some of his early ideas about the language:[[9]](#cite_note-9)

> I was talking with my colleague about the possibility of an object-oriented scripting language. I knew Perl (Perl4, not Perl5), but I didn't like it really, because it had the smell of a [toy language](/wiki/Toy_language "Toy language") (it still has). The object-oriented language seemed very promising. I knew [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)") then. But I didn't like it, because I didn't think it was a true object-oriented language – OO features appeared to be add-on to the language. As a language maniac and OO fan for 15 years, I really wanted a genuine object-oriented, easy-to-use scripting language. I looked for but couldn't find one. So I decided to make it.

Matsumoto described Ruby's design as resembling a simple [Lisp](/wiki/Lisp_(programming_language) "Lisp (programming language)") language at its core, with an object system like that of Smalltalk, blocks inspired by [higher-order functions](/wiki/Higher-order_function "Higher-order function"), and practical utility like that of Perl.[[10]](#cite_note-lisp-features-10)

The name "Ruby" originated during an online chat session between Matsumoto and Keiju Ishitsuka on 24 February 1993, before any code had been written.[[11]](#cite_note-rubyconf-history-of-ruby-11) Two names were initially proposed: "Coral" and "Ruby". Matsumoto chose the latter in a subsequent email to Ishitsuka.[[12]](#cite_note-ruby-name-12) He also noted that one factor influencing the choice of the name was that a colleague's [birthstone](/wiki/Birthstone "Birthstone") was [ruby](/wiki/Ruby_(gemstone) "Ruby (gemstone)").[[13]](#cite_note-faq-name-13)[[14]](#cite_note-ruby-talk-name-14)

### Early releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early releases")]

The first public release of Ruby 0.95 was announced on Japanese domestic [newsgroups](/wiki/Newsgroup "Newsgroup") on 21 December 1995.[[15]](#cite_note-0.95-15)[[16]](#cite_note-ruby-history-16) Subsequently, three more versions of Ruby were released in two days.[[11]](#cite_note-rubyconf-history-of-ruby-11) The release coincided with the launch of the [Japanese-language](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") *ruby-list* mailing list, which was the first mailing list for the new language.

Already present at this stage of development were many of the features familiar in later releases of Ruby, including [object-oriented](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming") design, [classes](/wiki/Class_(programming) "Class (programming)") with inheritance, [mixins](/wiki/Mixin "Mixin"), [iterators](/wiki/Iterator "Iterator"), [closures](/wiki/Closure_(computer_science) "Closure (computer science)"), [exception handling](/wiki/Exception_handling "Exception handling") and [garbage collection](/wiki/Garbage_collection_(computer_science) "Garbage collection (computer science)").[[17]](#cite_note-tutorial-features-17)

After the release of Ruby 0.95 in 1995, several stable versions of Ruby were released in these years.[[11]](#cite_note-rubyconf-history-of-ruby-11)

In 1997, the first article about Ruby was published on the Web. In the same year, Matsumoto was hired by [netlab.jp](/wiki/Network_Applied_Communication_Laboratory "Network Applied Communication Laboratory") to work on Ruby as a full-time developer.[[11]](#cite_note-rubyconf-history-of-ruby-11)

In 1998, the Ruby Application Archive was launched by Matsumoto, along with a simple English-language homepage for Ruby.[[11]](#cite_note-rubyconf-history-of-ruby-11)

In 1999, the first English language mailing list *ruby-talk* began, which signaled a growing interest in the language outside Japan.[[18]](#cite_note-linuxdevcenter-18) In this same year, Matsumoto and Keiju Ishitsuka wrote the first book on Ruby, *The Object-oriented Scripting Language Ruby* (オブジェクト指向スクリプト言語 Ruby), which was published in Japan in October 1999. It would be followed in the early 2000s by around 20 books on Ruby published in Japanese.[[11]](#cite_note-rubyconf-history-of-ruby-11)

By 2000, Ruby was more popular than Python in Japan.[[19]](#cite_note-programming-ruby-19) In September 2000, the first English language book *[Programming Ruby](/wiki/Programming_Ruby "Programming Ruby")* was printed, which was later freely released to the public, further widening the adoption of Ruby amongst English speakers. In early 2002, the English-language *ruby-talk* mailing list was receiving more messages than the Japanese-language *ruby-list*, demonstrating Ruby's increasing popularity in the non-Japanese speaking world.

### Ruby 1.8 and 1.9

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ruby 1.8 and 1.9")]

Ruby 1.8 was initially released August 2003, was stable for a long time, and was retired June 2013.[[20]](#cite_note-1.8.7-retirement-20) Although deprecated, there is still code based on it. Ruby 1.8 is only partially compatible with Ruby 1.9.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ruby 1.8 has been the subject of several industry standards. The language specifications for Ruby were developed by the Open Standards Promotion Center of the Information-Technology Promotion Agency (a [Japanese government](/wiki/Government_of_Japan "Government of Japan") agency) for submission to the [Japanese Industrial Standards Committee](/wiki/Japanese_Industrial_Standards_Committee "Japanese Industrial Standards Committee") (JISC) and then to the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO). It was accepted as a Japanese Industrial Standard (JIS X 3017) in 2011[[21]](#cite_note-JIS_X_3017-21) and an international standard (ISO/IEC 30170) in 2012.[[22]](#cite_note-IPA-22)[[23]](#cite_note-IEC_30170-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Ruby_On_Rails_Logo.svg/250px-Ruby_On_Rails_Logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ruby_On_Rails_Logo.svg)

[Ruby on Rails](/wiki/Ruby_on_Rails "Ruby on Rails") logo

Around 2005, interest in the Ruby language surged in tandem with [Ruby on Rails](/wiki/Ruby_on_Rails "Ruby on Rails"), a [web framework](/wiki/Web_framework "Web framework") written in Ruby. Rails is frequently credited with increasing awareness of Ruby.[[24]](#cite_note-Devarticles-24)

Effective with Ruby 1.9.3, released 31 October 2011,[[25]](#cite_note-Ruby_1.9.3-25) Ruby switched from being dual-licensed under the Ruby License and the GPL to being dual-licensed under the Ruby License and the two-clause BSD license.[[26]](#cite_note-license-change-26) Adoption of 1.9 was slowed by changes from 1.8 that required many popular third party [gems](#Repositories_and_libraries) to be rewritten.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Ruby 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ruby 2")]

Ruby 2.0 was intended to be fully backward compatible with Ruby 1.9.3. As of the official 2.0.0 release on 24 February 2013, there were only five known incompatibilities.[[27]](#cite_note-2-0-release-incompatibilities-27)

Starting with 2.1.0, Ruby's versioning policy changed to be more similar to [semantic versioning](/wiki/Semantic_versioning "Semantic versioning"), although it differs slightly in that minor version increments may be API incompatible.[[28]](#cite_note-semantic-versioning-28)

Ruby 2.2.0 includes speed-ups, bugfixes, and library updates and removes some deprecated APIs. Most notably, Ruby 2.2.0 introduces changes to memory handling – an incremental garbage collector, support for garbage collection of symbols and the option to compile directly against jemalloc. It also contains experimental support for using [vfork](/wiki/Vfork "Vfork")(2) with system() and spawn(), and added support for the [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") 7.0 specification. Since version 2.2.1,[[29]](#cite_note-2-2-1-release-29) [Ruby MRI](#Matz's_Ruby_interpreter) performance on [PowerPC64](/wiki/Ppc64 "Ppc64") was improved.[[30]](#cite_note-2-2-1-changelog-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Pedrosa-1-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Pedrosa-2-32) Features that were made obsolete or removed include callcc, the DL library, Digest::HMAC, lib/rational.rb, lib/complex.rb, GServer, Logger::Application as well as various C API functions.[[33]](#cite_note-obsolete-or-gone-in-2.2-33)

Ruby 2.3.0 includes many performance improvements, updates, and bugfixes including changes to Proc#call, Socket and IO use of exception keywords, Thread#name handling, default passive Net::FTP connections, and Rake being removed from stdlib.[[34]](#cite_note-Ruby_2.3.0_NEWS-34) Other notable changes include:

* The ability to mark all [string literals](/wiki/String_literal "String literal") as frozen by default with a consequently large performance increase in string operations.[[35]](#cite_note-frozen-strings-35)
* Hash comparison to allow direct checking of key/value pairs instead of just keys.
* A new [safe navigation operator](/wiki/Safe_navigation_operator "Safe navigation operator") `&.` that can ease nil handling (e.g. instead of `if obj && obj.foo && obj.foo.bar`, we can use `if obj&.foo&.bar`).
* The *did\_you\_mean* gem is now bundled by default and required on startup to automatically suggest similar name matches on a *NameError* or *NoMethodError*.
* *Hash#dig* and *Array#dig* to easily extract deeply nested values (e.g. given `profile = { social: { wikipedia: { name: 'Foo Baz' } } }`, the value *Foo Baz* can now be retrieved by `profile.dig(:social, :wikipedia, :name)`).
* `.grep_v(regexp)` which will match all negative examples of a given regular expression in addition to other new features.

Ruby 2.4.0 includes performance improvements to hash table, Array#max, Array#min, and instance variable access.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36) Other notable changes include:

* Binding#irb: Start a REPL session similar to binding.pry
* Unify *Fixnum* and *Bignum* into *Integer* class
* String supports Unicode case mappings, not just ASCII
* A new method, Regexp#match?, which is a faster Boolean version of Regexp#match
* Thread deadlock detection now shows threads with their backtrace and dependency

A few notable changes in Ruby 2.5.0 include *rescue* and *ensure* statements automatically use a surrounding *do-end* block (less need for extra *begin-end* blocks), method-chaining with *yield\_self*, support for branch coverage and method coverage measurement, and easier Hash transformations with *Hash#slice* and *Hash#transform\_keys* On top of that come a lot of performance improvements like faster block passing (3 times faster), faster Mutexes, faster ERB templates and improvements on some concatenation methods.

A few notable changes in Ruby 2.6.0 include an experimental [just-in-time compiler](/wiki/Just-in-time_compiler "Just-in-time compiler") (JIT), and *RubyVM::AbstractSyntaxTree* (experimental).

A few notable changes in Ruby 2.7.0 include pattern Matching (experimental), REPL improvements, a compaction GC, and separation of positional and keyword arguments.

### Ruby 3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ruby 3")]

Ruby 3.0.0 was released on [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") Day in 2020.[[37]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.0.0_Released-37) It is known as Ruby 3x3, which signifies that programs would run three times faster in Ruby 3.0 comparing to Ruby 2.0.[[38]](#cite_note-38) and some had already been implemented in intermediate releases on the road from 2 to 3. To achieve 3x3, Ruby 3 comes with MJIT, and later YJIT, Just-In-Time Compilers, to make programs faster, although they are described as experimental and remain disabled by default (enabled by flags at runtime).

Another goal of Ruby 3.0 is to improve [concurrency](/wiki/Concurrency_(computer_science) "Concurrency (computer science)") and two more utilities Fibre Scheduler, and experimental Ractor facilitate the goal.[[37]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.0.0_Released-37) Ractor is light-weight and thread-safe as it is achieved by exchanging messages rather than shared objects.

Ruby 3.0 introduces RBS language to describe the types of Ruby programs for [static analysis](/wiki/Static_program_analysis "Static program analysis").[[37]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.0.0_Released-37) It is separated from general Ruby programs.

There are some syntax enhancements and library changes in Ruby 3.0 as well.[[37]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.0.0_Released-37)

Ruby 3.1 was released on 25 December 2021.[[39]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.1.0_Released-39) It includes YJIT, a new, experimental, Just-In-Time Compiler developed by [Shopify](/wiki/Shopify "Shopify"), to enhance the performance of real world business applications. A new [debugger](/wiki/Debugger "Debugger") is also included. There are some syntax enhancements and other improvements in this release. Network libraries for [FTP](/wiki/FTP "FTP"), [SMTP](/wiki/SMTP "SMTP"), [IMAP](/wiki/IMAP "IMAP"), and [POP](/wiki/Post_Office_Protocol "Post Office Protocol") are moved from default gems to bundled gems.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Ruby 3.2 was released on 25 December 2022.[[41]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.2.0_Released-41) It brings support for being run inside of a [WebAssembly](/wiki/WebAssembly "WebAssembly") environment via a WASI interface. [Regular expressions](/wiki/Regular_expression "Regular expression") also receives some improvements, including a faster, [memoized](/wiki/Memoization "Memoization") matching algorithm to protect against certain [ReDoS](/wiki/ReDoS "ReDoS") attacks, and configurable timeouts for regular expression matching. Additional debugging and syntax features are also included in this release, which include syntax suggestion, as well as error highlighting. The MJIT compiler has been re-implemented as a standard library module, while the YJIT, a [Rust](/wiki/Rust_(programming_language) "Rust (programming language)")-based [JIT](/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation "Just-in-time compilation") compiler now supports more architectures on Linux.

Ruby 3.3 was released on 25 December 2023.[[42]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.3.0_Released-42) Ruby 3.3 introduces significant enhancements and performance improvements to the language. Key features include the introduction of the Prism parser for portable and maintainable parsing, the addition of the pure-Ruby JIT compiler RJIT, and major performance boosts in the YJIT compiler. Additionally, improvements in memory usage, the introduction of an M:N thread scheduler, and updates to the standard library contribute to a more efficient and developer-friendly Ruby ecosystem.

Ruby 3.4 was released on 25 December 2024.[[43]](#cite_note-Ruby_3.4.0_Released-43) Ruby 3.4 adds `it` block parameter reference, changes Prism as default parser, adds [Happy Eyeballs](/wiki/Happy_Eyeballs "Happy Eyeballs") Version 2 support to socket library, improves YJIT, adds modular Garbage Collector and so on.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Semantics and philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Semantics and philosophy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Yukihiro_Matsumoto.JPG/250px-Yukihiro_Matsumoto.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yukihiro_Matsumoto.JPG)

[Yukihiro Matsumoto](/wiki/Yukihiro_Matsumoto "Yukihiro Matsumoto"), the creator of Ruby

Matsumoto stated that Ruby is designed for programmer productivity and fun, following the principles of good [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") design.[[45]](#cite_note-informit-45) At a Google Tech Talk in 2008 he said, "I hope to see Ruby help every programmer in the world to be productive, and to enjoy programming, and to be happy. That is the primary purpose of Ruby language."[[46]](#cite_note-googletechtalk-46) He stresses that systems design needs to emphasize human, rather than computer, needs:[[47]](#cite_note-artima-47)

> Often people, especially computer engineers, focus on the machines. They think, "By doing this, the machine will run fast. By doing this, the machine will run more effectively. By doing this, the machine will something something something." They are focusing on machines. But in fact we need to focus on humans, on how humans care about doing programming or operating the application of the machines. We are the masters. They are the slaves.

Matsumoto has said his primary design goal was to make a language that he himself enjoyed using, by minimizing programmer work and possible confusion. He has said that he had not applied the [principle of least astonishment](/wiki/Principle_of_least_astonishment "Principle of least astonishment") (POLA) to the design of Ruby;[[47]](#cite_note-artima-47) in a May 2005 discussion on the newsgroup comp.lang.ruby, Matsumoto attempted to distance Ruby from POLA, explaining that because any design choice will be surprising to someone, he uses a personal standard in evaluating surprise. If that personal standard remains consistent, there would be few surprises for those familiar with the standard.[[48]](#cite_note-rubyweeklynews-48)

Matsumoto defined it this way in an interview:[[47]](#cite_note-artima-47)

> Everyone has an individual background. Someone may come from Python, someone else may come from Perl, and they may be surprised by different aspects of the language. Then they come up to me and say, 'I was surprised by this feature of the language, so Ruby violates the principle of least surprise.' Wait. Wait. The principle of least surprise is not for you only. The principle of least surprise means principle of least *my* surprise. And it means the principle of least surprise after you learn Ruby very well. For example, I was a C++ programmer before I started designing Ruby. I programmed in C++ exclusively for two or three years. And after two years of C++ programming, it still surprises me.

Ruby is [object-oriented](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming"): every value is an object, including classes and instances of types that many other languages designate as primitives (such as [integers](/wiki/Integer_(computer_science) "Integer (computer science)"), Booleans, and "[null](/wiki/Null_pointer "Null pointer")"). Because everything in Ruby is an object, everything in Ruby has certain built-in abilities called methods. Every [function](/wiki/Function_(programming) "Function (programming)") is a [method](/wiki/Method_(computer_programming) "Method (computer programming)") and methods are always called on an object. Methods defined at the top level scope become methods of the Object class. Since this class is an ancestor of every other class, such methods can be called on any object. They are also visible in all scopes, effectively serving as "global" procedures. Ruby supports [inheritance](/wiki/Inheritance_(object-oriented_programming) "Inheritance (object-oriented programming)") with [dynamic dispatch](/wiki/Dynamic_dispatch "Dynamic dispatch"), [mixins](/wiki/Mixin "Mixin") and singleton methods (belonging to, and defined for, a single [instance](/wiki/Instance_(computer_science) "Instance (computer science)") rather than being defined on the class). Though Ruby does not support [multiple inheritance](/wiki/Multiple_inheritance "Multiple inheritance"), classes can import [modules](/wiki/Modular_programming "Modular programming") as mixins.

Ruby has been described as a [multi-paradigm programming language](/wiki/Multi-paradigm_programming_language "Multi-paradigm programming language"): it allows procedural programming (defining functions/variables outside classes makes them part of the root, 'self' Object), with object orientation (everything is an object) or [functional programming](/wiki/Functional_programming "Functional programming") (it has [anonymous functions](/wiki/Anonymous_function "Anonymous function"), [closures](/wiki/Closure_(computer_science) "Closure (computer science)"), and [continuations](/wiki/Continuation "Continuation"); statements all have values, and functions return the last evaluation). It has support for [introspection](/wiki/Introspection_(computer_science) "Introspection (computer science)"), [reflective programming](/wiki/Reflective_programming "Reflective programming"), [metaprogramming](/wiki/Metaprogramming "Metaprogramming"), and interpreter-based [threads](/wiki/Thread_(computer_science) "Thread (computer science)"). Ruby features [dynamic typing](/wiki/Type_system "Type system"), and supports [parametric polymorphism](/wiki/Parametric_polymorphism "Parametric polymorphism").

According to the Ruby FAQ, the syntax is similar to [Perl](/wiki/Perl "Perl")'s and the semantics are similar to [Smalltalk's](/wiki/Smalltalk "Smalltalk"), but the design philosophy differs greatly from [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)")'s.[[49]](#cite_note-faq-comparison-49)

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Features")]

* Thoroughly [object-oriented](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming") with [inheritance](/wiki/Inheritance_(object-oriented_programming) "Inheritance (object-oriented programming)"), [mixins](/wiki/Mixin "Mixin") and [metaclasses](/wiki/Metaclass "Metaclass")[[50]](#cite_note-stewart-50)
* [Dynamic typing](/wiki/Dynamic_typing "Dynamic typing") and [duck typing](/wiki/Duck_typing "Duck typing")
* Everything is an [expression](/wiki/Expression_(programming) "Expression (programming)") (even [statements](/wiki/Statement_(programming) "Statement (programming)")) and everything is executed [imperatively](/wiki/Imperative_programming "Imperative programming") (even [declarations](/wiki/Declaration_(computer_science) "Declaration (computer science)"))
* Succinct and flexible syntax[[51]](#cite_note-venners-productivity-51) that minimizes [syntactic noise](/wiki/Syntactic_noise "Syntactic noise") and serves as a foundation for [domain-specific languages](/wiki/Domain-specific_languages "Domain-specific languages")[[52]](#cite_note-fowler-dsl-52)
* Dynamic [reflection](/wiki/Reflective_programming "Reflective programming") and [alteration](/wiki/Dynamic_programming_language#Object_runtime_alteration "Dynamic programming language") of objects to facilitate [metaprogramming](/wiki/Metaprogramming "Metaprogramming")[[53]](#cite_note-codeproject-dynamic-53)
* [Lexical closures](/wiki/Closure_(computer_science) "Closure (computer science)"), [iterators](/wiki/Iterator "Iterator") and [generators](/wiki/Generator_(computer_science) "Generator (computer science)"), with a [block syntax](/wiki/Ruby_syntax#Blocks_and_iterators "Ruby syntax")[[54]](#cite_note-venners-closures-54)
* Literal notation for [arrays](/wiki/Dynamic_array "Dynamic array"), [hashes](/wiki/Associative_array "Associative array"), [regular expressions](/wiki/Regular_expression "Regular expression") and [symbols](/wiki/Symbol_(Lisp) "Symbol (Lisp)")
* Embedding code in strings ([interpolation](/wiki/Variable_interpolation "Variable interpolation"))
* [Default arguments](/wiki/Default_argument "Default argument")
* Four levels of variable scope ([global](/wiki/Global_variable "Global variable"), [class](/wiki/Class_variable "Class variable"), [instance](/wiki/Instance_variable "Instance variable"), and [local](/wiki/Local_variable "Local variable")) denoted by [sigils](/wiki/Sigil_(computer_programming) "Sigil (computer programming)") or the lack thereof
* [Garbage collection](/wiki/Garbage_collection_(computer_science) "Garbage collection (computer science)")
* [First-class continuations](/wiki/First-class_continuation "First-class continuation")
* Strict Boolean [coercion](/wiki/Implicit_type_conversion "Implicit type conversion") rules (everything is *true* except `false` and `nil`)
* [Exception handling](/wiki/Exception_handling "Exception handling")
* [Operator overloading](/wiki/Operator_overloading "Operator overloading")[[55]](#cite_note-Methods-55)
* Built-in support for [rational numbers](/wiki/Rational_number "Rational number"), [complex numbers](/wiki/Complex_number "Complex number") and [arbitrary-precision arithmetic](/wiki/Arbitrary-precision_arithmetic "Arbitrary-precision arithmetic")
* Custom dispatch behavior (through `method_missing` and `const_missing`)
* Native [threads](/wiki/Thread_(computer_science) "Thread (computer science)") and cooperative [fibers](/wiki/Fiber_(computer_science) "Fiber (computer science)") (fibers are a 1.9/[YARV](/wiki/YARV "YARV") feature)
* Support for [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") and multiple [character encodings](/wiki/Character_encoding "Character encoding").
* Native [plug-in](/wiki/Plug-in_(computing) "Plug-in (computing)") API in [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)")
* Interactive Ruby Shell, an interactive command-line interpreter that can be used to test code quickly ([REPL](/wiki/Read%E2%80%93eval%E2%80%93print_loop "Read–eval–print loop"))
* Centralized package management through [RubyGems](/wiki/RubyGems "RubyGems")
* Implemented on all major platforms
* Large standard library, including modules for [YAML](/wiki/YAML "YAML"), [JSON](/wiki/JSON "JSON"), [XML](/wiki/XML "XML"), [CGI](/wiki/Common_Gateway_Interface "Common Gateway Interface"), [OpenSSL](/wiki/OpenSSL "OpenSSL"), [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP"), [FTP](/wiki/FTP "FTP"), [RSS](/wiki/RSS "RSS"), [curses](/wiki/Curses_(programming_library) "Curses (programming library)"), [zlib](/wiki/Zlib "Zlib") and [Tk](/wiki/Tk_(software) "Tk (software)")[[56]](#cite_note-stdlib-master-56)
* [Just-in-time compilation](/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation "Just-in-time compilation")

## Syntax

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Syntax")]

Main article: [Ruby syntax](/wiki/Ruby_syntax "Ruby syntax")

The syntax of Ruby is broadly similar to that of [Perl](/wiki/Perl "Perl") and [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)"). Class and method definitions are signaled by keywords, whereas code blocks can be defined by either keywords or braces. In contrast to Perl, variables are not obligatorily prefixed with a [sigil](/wiki/Sigil_(computer_programming) "Sigil (computer programming)"). When used, the sigil changes the semantics of scope of the variable. For practical purposes there is no distinction between [expressions](/wiki/Expression_(programming) "Expression (programming)") and [statements](/wiki/Statement_(programming) "Statement (programming)").[[57]](#cite_note-while-57)[[58]](#cite_note-precedence-58) Line breaks are significant and taken as the end of a statement; a semicolon may be equivalently used. Unlike Python, indentation is not significant.

One of the differences from Python and Perl is that Ruby keeps all of its instance variables completely private to the class and only exposes them through accessor methods (`attr_writer`, `attr_reader`, etc.). Unlike the "getter" and "setter" methods of other languages like [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++") or [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)"), accessor methods in Ruby can be created with a single line of code via [metaprogramming](/wiki/Metaprogramming "Metaprogramming"); however, accessor methods can also be created in the traditional fashion of C++ and Java. As invocation of these methods does not require the use of parentheses, it is trivial to change an instance variable into a full function, without modifying a single line of calling code or having to do any refactoring achieving similar functionality to [C#](/wiki/C_Sharp_(programming_language) "C Sharp (programming language)") and [VB.NET](/wiki/VB.NET "VB.NET") property members.

Python's property descriptors are similar, but come with a trade-off in the development process. If one begins in Python by using a publicly exposed instance variable, and later changes the implementation to use a private instance variable exposed through a property descriptor, code internal to the class may need to be adjusted to use the private variable rather than the public property. Ruby's design forces all instance variables to be private, but also provides a simple way to declare `set` and `get` methods. This is in keeping with the idea that in Ruby, one never directly accesses the internal members of a class from outside the class; rather, one passes a message to the class and receives a response.

## Implementations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Implementations")]

See also: [Ruby MRI § Operating systems](/wiki/Ruby_MRI#Operating_systems "Ruby MRI"), and [List of Ruby compilers](/wiki/List_of_Ruby_compilers "List of Ruby compilers")

### Matz's Ruby interpreter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Matz's Ruby interpreter")]

The original Ruby [interpreter](/wiki/Interpreter_(computer_software) "Interpreter (computer software)") is often referred to as [Matz's Ruby Interpreter](/wiki/Ruby_MRI "Ruby MRI") or MRI. This implementation is written in C and uses its own Ruby-specific [virtual machine](/wiki/Virtual_machine "Virtual machine").

The standardized and retired Ruby 1.8 [implementation](/wiki/Ruby_MRI "Ruby MRI") was written in [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)"), as a single-pass [interpreted language](/wiki/Interpreted_language "Interpreted language").[[20]](#cite_note-1.8.7-retirement-20)

Starting with Ruby 1.9, and continuing with Ruby 2.x and above, the official Ruby interpreter has been [YARV](/wiki/YARV "YARV") ("Yet Another Ruby VM"), and this implementation has superseded the slower virtual machine used in previous releases of MRI.

### Alternative implementations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Alternative implementations")]

As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit), there are a number of alternative implementations of Ruby, including [JRuby](/wiki/JRuby "JRuby"), [Rubinius](/wiki/Rubinius "Rubinius"), and [mruby](/wiki/Mruby "Mruby"). Each takes a different approach, with JRuby and Rubinius providing [just-in-time compilation](/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation "Just-in-time compilation") and mruby also providing [ahead-of-time compilation](/wiki/Ahead-of-time_compilation "Ahead-of-time compilation").

Ruby has three major alternative implementations:

* [JRuby](/wiki/JRuby "JRuby"), a mixed [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") and Ruby implementation that runs on the [Java virtual machine](/wiki/Java_virtual_machine "Java virtual machine"). JRuby currently targets Ruby 3.4.
* TruffleRuby, a Java implementation using the Truffle language implementation framework with [GraalVM](/wiki/GraalVM "GraalVM")
* [Rubinius](/wiki/Rubinius "Rubinius"), a [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++") [bytecode](/wiki/Bytecode "Bytecode") virtual machine that uses [LLVM](/wiki/LLVM "LLVM") to compile to machine code at runtime. The bytecode compiler and most core classes are written in pure Ruby. Rubinius currently targets Ruby 2.3.1.

Other Ruby implementations include:

* [MagLev](/wiki/MagLev_(software) "MagLev (software)"), a [Smalltalk](/wiki/Smalltalk "Smalltalk") implementation that runs on [GemTalk Systems](/wiki/GemTalk_Systems "GemTalk Systems")' [GemStone/S](/wiki/Gemstone_(database) "Gemstone (database)") VM
* [mruby](/wiki/Mruby "Mruby"), an implementation designed to be embedded into C code, in a similar vein to [Lua](/wiki/Lua "Lua"). It is being developed by [Yukihiro Matsumoto](/wiki/Yukihiro_Matsumoto "Yukihiro Matsumoto") and others
* [RGSS](/wiki/RGSS "RGSS"), or Ruby Game Scripting System, a [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") implementation used by the [RPG Maker](/wiki/RPG_Maker "RPG Maker") series of [role-playing game creation software](/wiki/Role-playing_game_creation_software "Role-playing game creation software") for game design and modification of the RPG Maker [game engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine")
* julializer, a [transpiler](/wiki/Transpiler "Transpiler") (partial) from Ruby to [Julia](/wiki/Julia_(programming_language) "Julia (programming language)"). It can be used for a large speedup over e.g. Ruby or JRuby implementations (may only be useful for numerical code).[[59]](#cite_note-virtual-module-59)
* Topaz, a Ruby implementation written in [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)")
* Opal, a web-based interpreter that compiles Ruby to [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript")

Other now defunct Ruby implementations were:

* [MacRuby](/wiki/MacRuby "MacRuby"), a [Mac OS X](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS") implementation on the [Objective-C](/wiki/Objective-C "Objective-C") runtime. Its iOS counterpart is called [RubyMotion](/wiki/RubyMotion "RubyMotion")
* [IronRuby](/wiki/IronRuby "IronRuby") an implementation on the [.NET Framework](/wiki/.NET_Framework ".NET Framework")
* Cardinal, an implementation for the [Parrot virtual machine](/wiki/Parrot_virtual_machine "Parrot virtual machine")
* [Ruby Enterprise Edition](/wiki/Ruby_Enterprise_Edition "Ruby Enterprise Edition"), often shortened to *ree*, an implementation optimized to handle large-scale [Ruby on Rails](/wiki/Ruby_on_Rails "Ruby on Rails") projects
* [HotRuby](/wiki/HotRuby "HotRuby"), a [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript") and [ActionScript](/wiki/ActionScript "ActionScript") implementation of the Ruby programming language

The maturity of Ruby implementations tends to be measured by their ability to run the [Ruby on Rails](/wiki/Ruby_on_Rails "Ruby on Rails") (Rails) framework, because it is complex to implement and uses many Ruby-specific features. The point when a particular implementation achieves this goal is called "the Rails singularity". The reference implementation, JRuby, and Rubinius[[60]](#cite_note-Rubinius-60) are all able to run Rails unmodified in a production environment.

### Platform support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Platform support")]

Matsumoto originally developed Ruby on the [4.3BSD](/wiki/BSD "BSD")-based [Sony NEWS-OS](/wiki/Sony_NEWS "Sony NEWS") 3.x, but later migrated his work to [SunOS](/wiki/SunOS "SunOS") 4.x, and finally to [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux").[[61]](#cite_note-Stodte-61)[[62]](#cite_note-initial-development-62) By 1999, Ruby was known to work across many different [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system"). Modern Ruby versions and implementations are available on all major desktop, mobile and server-based operating systems. Ruby is also supported across a number of cloud hosting platforms like [Jelastic](/wiki/Jelastic "Jelastic"), [Heroku](/wiki/Heroku "Heroku"), [Google Cloud Platform](/wiki/Google_Cloud_Platform "Google Cloud Platform") and others.

Tools such as [RVM](/wiki/Ruby_Version_Manager "Ruby Version Manager") and RBEnv, facilitate installation and partitioning of multiple ruby versions, and multiple 'gemsets' on one machine.

## Repositories and libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruby_(programming_language)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Repositories and libraries")]

[RubyGems](/wiki/RubyGems "RubyGems") is Ruby's package manager. A Ruby package is called a "gem" and can be installed via the command line. Most gems are libraries, though a few exist that are applications, such as [IDEs](/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment").[[63]](#cite_note-ruby-toolbox-63) There are over 100,000 Ruby gems hosted on [RubyGems.org](https://rubygems.org/).[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Many new and existing Ruby libraries are hosted on [GitHub](/wiki/GitHub "GitHub"), a service that offers [version control](/wiki/Revision_control "Revision control") repository hosting for [Git](/wiki/Git "Git").

The Ruby Application Archive, which hosted applications, documentation, and libraries for Ruby programming, was maintained until 2013, when its function was transferred to RubyGems.[[65]](#cite_note-raa-retirement-65)
